function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
What specific language does the filing use regarding timing of Starship commercial payload delivery, V3 satellite deployment, and V2 Mobile deployment? Compare the wording and assess what it suggests about execution and timing risk.

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about specific language in the SpaceX S-1 filing regarding timing of:
1. Starship commercial payload delivery
2. V3 satellite deployment
3. V2 Mobile deployment

I need to parse the filing and then retrieve the specific language used for each of these items. Let me start by parsing the HTML page.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve information about each of these three topics from the filing.

arguments.prompt: What specific language does the filing use regarding the timing of Starship commercial payload delivery? Retrieve exact quotes and forward-looking statements about Starship commercial operations timeline in {{spacex_s1}}
What specific language does the filing use regarding V3 satellite deployment timing? Retrieve exact quotes about V3 satellite constellation deployment schedule and timeline in {{spacex_s1}}
What specific language does the filing use regarding V2 Mobile deployment timing? Retrieve exact quotes about V2 Mobile satellite deployment schedule and timeline in {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided prospectus chunks, the filing uses specific, repeated language regarding the timeline for Starship's commercial payload delivery, explicitly tying it to the **second half of 2026**. 

Here are the exact quotes and forward-looking statements regarding the Starship commercial operations timeline, categorized by their context within the filing:

### 1. Core Timeline for Payload Delivery
The prospectus repeatedly establishes the primary target for commercial operations with the following exact phrase across multiple sections (including the Preamble, Growth Strategy, and Starship Overview):
* *"We expect Starship to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026."*

### 2. Forward-Looking Statements Classification
In the "Cautionary Statement Regarding Forward-Looking Statements" section, the filing legally classifies the 2026 payload delivery timeline as a forward-looking projection subject to risks:
* *"Forward-looking statements contained in this prospectus also include, but are not limited to, statements about: • the development and deployment of Starship in accordance with our anticipated schedule (including commencement of payload delivery to orbit in 2026) and launch cadence and our ability to achieve expected performance, reusability, and cost efficiencies;"*

### 3. Financial & Commercialization Milestones
The filing links the timeline of commercial payload delivery to a shift in how the company will handle accounting, R&D expenses, and cost capitalization:
* *"We expect Space research and development to increase both in absolute dollars and as a percentage of revenue in 2026, as we invest in the development and commercialization of Starship, and to moderate both in absolute dollars and as a percentage of revenue once Starship is commercialized by delivering payload to orbit."*
* *"At commercialization, Starship costs generally will be capitalized and then depreciated in cost of revenue of the segment associated with the payload delivered."*

### 4. Dependencies and Expectations
The text notes that the company's broader growth strategy and revenue recognition are heavily dependent on this specific timeline:
* *"This includes our expectations with respect to completion of flight testing of Starship and commencement of payload delivery to orbit."*
* *"If we are unable to achieve the commercial development, anticipated performance, launch cadence, or cost efficiencies associated with Starship within expected timeframes, our ability to deploy next-generation V3 satellites, V2 Mobile satellites, and orbital AI compute infrastructure at scale... could be materially and adversely affected."*

### 5. Risk Disclosures & Caveats to the Timeline
The filing includes extensive warnings that the anticipated 2026 schedule is not guaranteed and outlines the consequences of missing these commercialization targets:
* *"If we are unable to successfully complete the development, testing, and deployment of Starship at scale in accordance with our anticipated schedule, or at all... our ability to execute our growth strategy... would be materially and adversely affected."*
* *"Any significant delay in achieving key development milestones, obtaining the necessary regulatory approvals or increasing and maintaining our launch cadence, reusability, and capability would impede the expansion of our service offerings, defer anticipated revenue streams, and negatively impact our growth trajectory and competitive positioning in rapidly evolving markets."*
* *"Delays or challenges in the Starship program have in the past occurred, and may occur in the future due to a variety of factors, including unforeseen technical challenges, supply chain disruptions, manufacturing difficulties, delays in the development, construction or commissioning of launch and fueling infrastructure... regulatory hurdles, or the need for additional design modifications."*

Based on the provided text from the prospectus, the filing repeatedly targets the **second half of 2026** for the deployment of the next-generation V3 satellite constellation, while heavily conditioning this timeline on the developmental and regulatory schedule of the Starship launch vehicle. 

Here are the exact quotes regarding the V3 satellite deployment schedule, timeline, and associated dependencies, categorized by their context in the filing:

### 1. Core Deployment Timeline & Schedule
The filing explicitly states the targeted timeline for V3 deployment across multiple sections (Prospectus Summary, Business Overview, and Growth Strategy):
* *"We expect to commence deploying our next-generation V3 satellites, designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite, using Starship in the second half of 2026."*
* *"...and in the second half of 2026, we expect to begin deployment of our next-generation V3 satellites, each of which is designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite."*
* *"“V3 satellites” refers to our next-generation Starlink broadband satellites, which are designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite and which we expect to begin deploying on Starship in the second half of"* *(Note: The provided text fragment cuts off exactly here before the year "2026" can be printed in the definitions section).*

### 2. Timeline Dependencies & Schedule Risks (Starship Development)
The Risk Factors section explicitly ties the V3 deployment schedule to Starship's testing, regulatory approvals, and launch cadence, warning that delays to Starship will delay V3:
* *"If we are unable to successfully complete the development, testing, and deployment of Starship at scale in accordance with our anticipated schedule, or at all... our ability to execute our growth strategy (such as the deployment of our next-generation V3 satellites, V2 satellite-to-mobile connectivity, and providing orbital AI compute infrastructure) would be materially and adversely affected."*
* *"Any significant delay in achieving key development milestones, obtaining the necessary regulatory approvals or increasing and maintaining our launch cadence... would impede the expansion of our service offerings, defer anticipated revenue streams, and negatively impact our growth trajectory..."*
* *"If we are unable to achieve the commercial development, anticipated performance, launch cadence, or cost efficiencies associated with Starship within expected timeframes, our ability to deploy next-generation V3 satellites... at scale... could be materially and adversely affected."*
* *"Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites..."*

### 3. Deployment Scale & Cadence Expectations
The filing outlines how the timeline will be executed once Starship becomes operational, noting the massive increase in deployment speed compared to current schedules:
* *"We expect that a single Starship launch will be capable of deploying up to 60 V3 satellites to LEO, representing a potential twenty-fold increase in Starlink downlink capacity deployed relative to a Falcon 9 launch."*
* *"Starship is a key enabler of our growth objectives, including the deployment of next-generation V3 satellites... Achieving our targeted launch cadence with Starship will require significant progress on several key milestones... and obtaining the necessary regulatory approvals, particularly from the FAA, to support a high launch cadence..."*
* *"Full reusability of Starship’s upper stage is not required to deploy our V3 satellites... in low-Earth orbit... meaningful advancement across the deployment of next-generation V3 satellites... is not dependent on achieving full reusability."*

### 4. Design Readiness & Orbital AI Timeline Synergy
The filing notes that the V3 platform is already fully designed and will act as the foundation for other upcoming deployments (like Orbital AI compute satellites):
* *"Specifically, we expect these satellites to leverage our already-designed V3 satellite platform. The core V3 satellite design is complete..."*
* *"Our V3 satellite platform already incorporates proprietary chips, providing a strong foundation for the ability to operate AI-focused electronics in space, and we expect to begin deploying our orbital AI compute satellites as"* *(Note: Text fragment cuts off).*

Based on the provided prospectus text, the filing specifies **2027** as the target year for deploying the V2 Mobile satellites, while also outlining several critical dependencies (regulatory, spectrum acquisition, and launch vehicle capabilities) that dictate the actual schedule and timeline for commercialization. 

Here are the exact quotes regarding the V2 Mobile deployment timing, schedule, and prerequisites:

### 1. Specific Deployment Timeline (2027)
The filing explicitly states the target year for deploying the next-generation V2 Mobile constellation:
> "We plan to expand our mobile constellation by deploying our next-generation mobile V2 Mobile satellites in **2027** which, combined with the EchoStar spectrum acquisition and optimized 5G protocols, are expected to increase capacity by orders of magnitude compared to our first-generation constellation."

### 2. Schedule Dependencies & Commercialization Prerequisites
The timeline for actually launching the commercial "Gen2" service is contingent upon the satellites being in orbit alongside the closing of the EchoStar spectrum acquisition and regulatory approvals (expected in late 2026 to 2027):
> "...we expect to receive the remaining necessary U.S. regulatory authorizations in the second or third quarter of 2026. While these authorizations would be sufficient from a U.S. regulatory perspective, **we still require our V2 Mobile satellites to be in orbit and must complete the acquisition of the relevant spectrum from EchoStar before we can commence our planned commercial Gen2 service** in the United States."

> "We expect the spectrum acquisition to close in November 2027, subject to required regulatory approvals and other closing conditions."

### 3. Launch Vehicle Constraints Impacting the Schedule
The filing notes that the deployment timeline is strictly bound to the development and readiness of the Starship launch vehicle, as current rockets cannot accommodate the V2 Mobile satellites:
> "Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, **are not capable of deploying** V3 satellites and **V2 Mobile satellites**."

> "Full reusability of Starship’s upper stage **is not required to deploy** our V3 satellites and **V2 Mobile satellites** in low-Earth orbit... Starship’s substantial payload capacity to LEO, even in partially reusable or expendable configurations, enables meaningful progress toward these objectives." 

> "...our ability to deploy next-generation V3 satellites, **V2 Mobile satellites**, and orbital AI compute infrastructure at scale... would be materially and adversely affected [if we are unable to successfully complete the development, testing, and deployment of Starship at scale in accordance with our anticipated schedule]."

steps.reasoning_content: I now have detailed information about all three topics. Let me also retrieve any additional language about execution risk and timing risk to provide a thorough comparison.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve exact language about Starship development risks, delays, technical challenges, and regulatory hurdles that could affect timing in {{spacex_s1}}
Retrieve exact language about "second half of 2026" and any qualifying or conditional language around that timeline in {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided text from the S-1 prospectus preamble and risk factors, here is the exact language detailing the development risks, delays, technical challenges, and regulatory hurdles that could affect Starship's timing and deployment:

### 1. General Development Risks & Unforeseen Delays
* "Unexpected design modifications, supply chain disruptions, anomalies, environmental issues, and other unforeseen technical challenges could result in delays or failures to deploy Starship on our anticipated schedule..."
* "The commercial deployment of Starship, particularly at scale, is subject to substantial risks and uncertainties inherent in the development of new and complex technologies and systems."
* "Delays or challenges in the Starship program have in the past occurred, and may occur in the future due to a variety of factors, including unforeseen technical challenges, supply chain disruptions, manufacturing difficulties... loss or damage to the vehicle or other components, regulatory hurdles, or the need for additional design modifications."
* "If we are required to undertake unanticipated redesigns, conduct additional testing, replace lost vehicles or components, or address operational setbacks, we may experience delays and incur significant additional costs, or be forced to reallocate critical resources from other projects."
* "Our rocket programs have historically required substantial time and resources to reach the cadence and cost thresholds necessary for commercial viability, and the development of Starship may face similar or greater challenges."

### 2. Technical & Engineering Challenges
* "We face a number of material challenges and uncertainties in achieving these milestones, such as achieving reliable high-cadence return-to-launch-site operations for the full vehicle stack, developing durable reusable heat shields capable of withstanding repeated high-velocity reentries, ensuring rapid refurbishment and high-rate reusability of engines and other vehicle components..."
* "Orbital refueling involves technical complexities associated with cryogenic propellant transfer in microgravity, propellant settling, and boil-off management and is required for lunar and interplanetary objectives."
* "These systems involve significant technological, engineering, and operational challenges, including the need to develop habitable transportation and surface environments, and perform complex in-orbit operations. Solving these challenges will require developing solutions that are novel or untested and will require substantial capital investment."
* "Launch vehicle underperformance, propulsion anomalies, structural failures, software errors, or other malfunctions could result in launch delays or partial or total mission failures, including the loss of satellites or payloads."

### 3. Infrastructure, Manufacturing & Pre-Launch Hurdles
* "...delays in the development, construction or commissioning of launch and fueling infrastructure (such as launch pads, air separation units and other propellant production systems), unavailability of such launch and fueling infrastructure (including launch pads) in sufficient number and in operable condition (including as a result of mishaps)..."
* "If our launch pads are not available for an extended period of time for any reason, we may not be able to achieve our development, testing and deployment goals. Such delays could have cascading effects on our ability to achieve the scale we need to timely achieve future objectives."
* "Our launch vehicles, satellites, and related systems have in the past experienced and may in the future experience delays, damage or destruction during design and manufacturing, including delays in fabrication, assembly, inspection, testing, and component qualification."
* "Our operations also involve significant risks during pre-launch preparation. Launch vehicles and satellites can be damaged or destroyed during transport, fueling, integration, or ground testing."
* "Even minor anomalies may require extensive troubleshooting or repairs, resulting in launch delays, increased mission costs, or the loss of flight hardware."

### 4. Regulatory & FAA Hurdles
* "...navigating complex and evolving regulatory requirements and environmental and technological issues as we seek to increase our launch cadence."
* "...obtaining the necessary regulatory approvals, particularly from the FAA, to support a high launch cadence while addressing public safety and environmental considerations."
* "...managing public and regulatory tolerance for anomalies during the transition to frequent operational flights... and obtaining timely regulatory approvals from the FAA and other agencies."
* "For example, current FAA regulations do not permit return-to-launch-site reentries for Starship, requiring us to obtain a waiver from the FAA, which is not guaranteed and could delay or restrict such operations."
* "Following an anomaly, mishap, or failure, the FAA or other authorities may require investigations, impose corrective actions, or restrict or delay our ability to conduct launch operations. We have in the past been, and may in the future become, subject to such actions, impacting our ability to increase launch cadence."

### 5. Reusability & Economic Viability Complexities
* "If Starship does not achieve full reusability or rapid turnaround, we may experience higher per-launch costs, slower deployment timelines for our large-scale constellations (including our orbital AI compute program), delayed revenue growth, and increased overall capital requirements, and our brand and reputation may suffer."
* "AI compute satellites at scale need full Starship reusability to be economically compelling. Without full reusability and rapid turnaround, Starship would still be capable of enabling progress on our next-generation Starlink, direct-to-cell, initial lunar objectives, and early AI compute satellite deployments, but such progress would be at a slower pace and higher cost."

### 6. Cascading Impacts on Timelines & Strategic Objectives
* "Any failure or delay in the development of Starship at scale or in achieving the required launch cadence, reusability and capabilities thereafter would delay or limit our ability to execute our growth strategy, including the deployment of next-generation satellites, global satellite-to-mobile connectivity, and orbital AI compute..."
* "Any significant delay in achieving key development milestones, obtaining the necessary regulatory approvals or increasing and maintaining our launch cadence, reusability, and capability would impede the expansion of our service offerings, defer anticipated revenue streams, and negatively impact our growth trajectory and competitive positioning in rapidly evolving markets."
* "Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites."
* "This includes our expectations with respect to completion of flight testing of Starship and commencement of payload delivery to orbit... We expect Starship to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026 following additional flight tests."
* "If these efforts take longer than anticipated, or if technical, operational, or engineering challenges arise in connection with these efforts, our goals with respect to the Moon and Mars, including government contracts, and other and multiplanetary initiatives could be delayed, modified, or cancelled..."

Based on the provided text, the timeline for the **"second half of 2026"** is associated with two major, interdependent milestones: the commencement of Starship's orbital payload delivery and the deployment of next-generation V3 Starlink satellites. 

Here is the exact language and the qualifying/conditional language surrounding these timelines:

### 1. Starship Payload Delivery to Orbit
**Exact Language:**
* *"We expect Starship to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026."*
* *"We expect Starship to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026 following additional flight tests."*

**Qualifying & Conditional Language:**
* **Forward-Looking Qualifier:** The use of **"We expect"** frames the timeline as an anticipation rather than a guaranteed date.
* **Operational Prerequisite:** The timeline is explicitly conditioned on the completion of **"following additional flight tests."** 
* **Explicit Risk/Delay Conditions:** The text immediately follows the timeline with a cross-reference to developmental risks that could alter the schedule: *"For additional information about this risk, please refer to 'Risk Factors —Risks Related to Our Business—**Any failure or delay in the development of Starship at scale or in achieving the required launch cadence, reusability and capabilities thereafter would delay or limit our ability to execute our growth strategy...'"***

### 2. Deployment of Next-Generation V3 Satellites
**Exact Language:**
* *"We expect to commence deploying our next-generation V3 satellites, designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite, using Starship in the second half of 2026..."*
* *"...and in the second half of 2026, we expect to begin deployment of our next-generation V3 satellites, each of which is designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite."*

**Qualifying & Conditional Language:**
* **Forward-Looking Qualifiers:** Phrases like **"We expect to commence deploying"** and **"we expect to begin"** indicate that this is a targeted goal subject to change.
* **Technological Dependency:** The deployment is strictly conditioned on the successful operationalization of the new rocket, noted by the phrase **"using Starship."** Therefore, the V3 deployment timeline is entirely dependent on Starship achieving its own "second half of 2026" payload delivery milestone.
* **Performance Expectations:** The text attaches further forward-looking conditions to the *results* of these deployments: *"...and **expect that** a single Starship launch **will be capable of** deploying up to 60 V3 satellites to LEO, representing a potential twenty-fold increase in Starlink downlink capacity deployed relative to a Falcon 9 launch."*

### Summary of Conditions
The "second half of 2026" timeline is heavily caveated. It is not a fixed date but a **forward-looking expectation ("we expect")** that is strictly **conditional upon the successful execution of prerequisite "additional flight tests."** Furthermore, the company explicitly warns that any "failure or delay" in achieving Starship's reusability, scale, or launch cadence will directly delay this timeline and the subsequent deployment of the V3 satellite constellation.

steps.message: Now I have all the specific language needed. Let me compile the comprehensive comparison and risk assessment.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have comprehensive information about all three topics. Let me compile a thorough comparison of the specific language used for each, and assess what it suggests about execution and timing risk.

Key findings:
1. **Starship commercial payload delivery**: "We expect Starship to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026" - uses "expect" and "commence"
2. **V3 satellite deployment**: "We expect to commence deploying our next-generation V3 satellites...using Starship in the second half of 2026" - also "expect" and "commence deploying"
3. **V2 Mobile deployment**: "We plan to expand our mobile constellation by deploying our next-generation mobile V2 Mobile satellites in 2027" - uses "plan" and "deploying" (not "commence deploying")

The differences in language are significant:
- Starship and V3 both use "expect" and target "second half of 2026"
- V2 Mobile uses "plan" (weaker than "expect") and targets "2027" (a full year, not a half-year)
- V3 is explicitly dependent on Starship ("using Starship")
- V2 Mobile has additional dependencies: EchoStar spectrum acquisition (expected to close November 2027), regulatory authorizations, AND Starship

Let me now write a comprehensive analysis.

arguments.final_result:
## Comparison of Timing Language: Starship Payload Delivery, V3 Satellite Deployment, and V2 Mobile Deployment

### 1. Starship Commercial Payload Delivery

**Exact Language Used:**
- *"We expect Starship to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026."*
- *"We expect Starship to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026 following additional flight tests."*

**Key Linguistic Features:**
- **Verb:** "expect" — a forward-looking statement of anticipation, not a commitment or guarantee.
- **Action verb:** "commence" — indicates the *start* of a process, not completion or scale.
- **Timeframe:** "second half of 2026" — a six-month window (July–December 2026), not a specific date.
- **Explicit prerequisite:** "following additional flight tests" — the timeline is expressly conditioned on completing further testing milestones.

---

### 2. V3 Satellite Deployment

**Exact Language Used:**
- *"We expect to commence deploying our next-generation V3 satellites, designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite, using Starship in the second half of 2026."*
- *"...and in the second half of 2026, we expect to begin deployment of our next-generation V3 satellites, each of which is designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite."*

**Key Linguistic Features:**
- **Verb:** "expect" — same forward-looking qualifier as Starship.
- **Action verb:** "commence deploying" / "begin deployment" — again, only the *start* of deployment, not full constellation buildout.
- **Timeframe:** "second half of 2026" — identical window to Starship payload delivery.
- **Explicit dependency:** "using Starship" — V3 deployment is structurally coupled to Starship's operational readiness. The filing further states: *"Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites."*
- **Design readiness:** The filing notes *"The core V3 satellite design is complete,"* suggesting the satellite-side risk is lower than the launch-vehicle-side risk.

---

### 3. V2 Mobile Satellite Deployment

**Exact Language Used:**
- *"We plan to expand our mobile constellation by deploying our next-generation mobile V2 Mobile satellites in 2027 which, combined with the EchoStar spectrum acquisition and optimized 5G protocols, are expected to increase capacity by orders of magnitude compared to our first-generation constellation."*

**Key Linguistic Features:**
- **Verb:** "plan" — a notably **weaker** forward-looking qualifier than "expect." A "plan" connotes intention; an "expectation" connotes a higher degree of confidence.
- **Action verb:** "deploying" — no "commence" or "begin" qualifier, which could imply a broader deployment effort rather than just initiation, though the year-level timeframe is less precise.
- **Timeframe:** "2027" — a full calendar year, not a half-year window. This is the **least precise** timing disclosure of the three.
- **Multiple explicit dependencies:** The deployment is conditioned on:
  1. **Starship readiness** (same as V3): *"Our current operational rockets...are not capable of deploying...V2 Mobile satellites."*
  2. **EchoStar spectrum acquisition:** *"We expect the spectrum acquisition to close in November 2027, subject to required regulatory approvals and other closing conditions."*
  3. **Regulatory authorizations:** *"...we expect to receive the remaining necessary U.S. regulatory authorizations in the second or third quarter of 2026...we still require our V2 Mobile satellites to be in orbit and must complete the acquisition of the relevant spectrum from EchoStar before we can commence our planned commercial Gen2 service in the United States."*

---

## Comparative Risk Assessment

| Dimension | Starship Payload Delivery | V3 Satellite Deployment | V2 Mobile Deployment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidence verb** | "expect" | "expect" | "plan" (weaker) |
| **Action scope** | "commence" (initiation only) | "commence deploying" / "begin deployment" (initiation only) | "deploying" (no "commence" qualifier) |
| **Timeframe precision** | "second half of 2026" (6-month window) | "second half of 2026" (6-month window) | "2027" (12-month window) |
| **Primary dependency** | Additional flight tests | Starship operational readiness | Starship + EchoStar spectrum + regulatory approvals |
| **Number of critical-path dependencies** | 1 (flight tests) | 2 (Starship + satellite manufacturing) | 3+ (Starship + spectrum acquisition + regulatory) |
| **Fallback option if Starship delayed** | None | None (Falcon 9/Heavy cannot deploy V3) | None (Falcon 9/Heavy cannot deploy V2 Mobile) |

### What the Language Suggests About Execution and Timing Risk

**1. Cascading single-point-of-failure risk.** All three milestones are structurally dependent on Starship. The filing is explicit: *"Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites."* This means any delay to Starship automatically delays both V3 and V2 Mobile, with no fallback launch vehicle. The filing acknowledges this directly: *"If we are unable to achieve the commercial development, anticipated performance, launch cadence, or cost efficiencies associated with Starship within expected timeframes, our ability to deploy next-generation V3 satellites, V2 Mobile satellites, and orbital AI compute infrastructure at scale...could be materially and adversely affected."*

**2. Deliberately graduated confidence levels.** The shift from "expect" (Starship and V3) to "plan" (V2 Mobile) is analytically meaningful. In SEC disclosure drafting, "expect" implies a reasonable basis for the stated anticipation, while "plan" signals an intention that is more contingent on external factors. V2 Mobile's weaker verb aligns with its greater number of external dependencies (spectrum acquisition closing, regulatory approvals) that are partially outside SpaceX's direct control.

**3. "Commence" vs. scale.** Both Starship payload delivery and V3 deployment use "commence" or "begin," which signals only the *initiation* of operations — not commercial scale, regular cadence, or revenue materiality. The filing separately warns that achieving the *cadence* needed for meaningful constellation deployment is a distinct and additional milestone: *"Achieving our targeted launch cadence with Starship will require significant progress on several key milestones...and obtaining the necessary regulatory approvals, particularly from the FAA."*

**4. Historical acknowledgment of delays.** The filing explicitly states: *"Delays or challenges in the Starship program have in the past occurred, and may occur in the future."* This is a direct admission that the stated timelines are aspirational targets that have already slipped historically, increasing the probability of further slippage.

**5. V2 Mobile has the highest timing risk.** With three independent critical-path dependencies (Starship readiness, EchoStar spectrum acquisition expected to close in November 2027, and remaining regulatory authorizations), V2 Mobile faces compounding execution risk. Even if Starship achieves payload delivery in H2 2026, the EchoStar spectrum acquisition is not expected to close until November 2027, meaning commercial Gen2 service cannot begin until late 2027 at the earliest — and only if all three dependencies are simultaneously satisfied.

**6. Partial reusability as a mitigant.** The filing provides one important mitigating disclosure: *"Full reusability of Starship's upper stage is not required to deploy our V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites in low-Earth orbit...Starship's substantial payload capacity to LEO, even in partially reusable or expendable configurations, enables meaningful progress toward these objectives."* This suggests that even if full reusability (needed for economic viability of orbital AI compute) is delayed, V3 and V2 Mobile deployment could still proceed at higher per-launch cost.

### Conclusion

The filing's language constructs a clear hierarchy of timing confidence: Starship payload delivery and V3 deployment are presented with roughly equivalent (moderate) confidence as near-term "expectations" for H2 2026, while V2 Mobile is presented with lower confidence as a "plan" for 2027 with materially more external dependencies. All three share a common single-point-of-failure in Starship, and the filing's own risk disclosures acknowledge that delays "have in the past occurred" and could cascade across the entire growth strategy. Investors should interpret these timelines as aspirational targets subject to significant execution risk, not as committed delivery dates.

---

**Sources:**

  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026)"
    }
  ]
}
